news_summary: Today's environmental news highlights effective public investment in waste collection, strengthened EU measures against corporate pollution and carbon emissions, and innovative efforts to protect ecosystems from invasive species.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des investissements publics efficaces dans la collecte des déchets, le renforcement des mesures de l'UE contre la pollution industrielle et les émissions de carbone, ainsi que des initiatives innovantes visant à protéger les écosystèmes contre les espèces envahissantes.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan la eficacia de la inversión pública en la recogida de residuos, el refuerzo de las medidas de la UE contra la contaminación industrial y las emisiones de carbono, y las iniciativas innovadoras para proteger los ecosistemas frente a las especies invasoras.

articles.title: Waste Collection is the Public Investment That Pays for Itself
articles.summary:
The U.S. recycling industry generates $184 billion annually and supports more than 603,000 jobs built...
The post Waste Collection is the Public Investment That Pays for Itself appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
The U.S. recycling industry generates $184 billion annually and supports more than 603,000 jobs built on infrastructure that the federal government estimates is only half-ready for the growing waste challenge as it needs to be.

That gap between what the system does and what it could do represents one of the clearest unspent investments in the American economy. The EPA’s 2024 recycling infrastructure assessment puts the cost of closing it at $36.5 to $43.4 billion, which the agency estimated was enough to raise the national recycling rate from 32% to 61% and recover an additional 82 to 89 million tons of material every year.

The U.S. currently spends more than $250 billion annually managing solid waste. An investment in better collection and processing can pay for itself quickly, and then keep paying dividends in cash and reduced environmental impacts.

Households Can’t Recycle as Easily as Trashing Waste

Before the economics, let’s look at the experience in communities: access to recycling in the United States is neither universal nor equitable. The EPA found that 6% of homes have no recycling service. Roughly 40% lack recycling services that are meaningfully equivalent to trash pickup; they can’t set a bin at the curb and have it collected on a schedule.

Rural and lower-income communities are disproportionately underserved. Households without curbside collection must drive materials to drop-off sites, which may be a realistic option for some, but acts as an effective barrier for others. The result is that the 32% national recycling rate reflects not just individual behavior, but the infrastructure decisions (and non-decisions) that preceded it.

The reality of U.S. waste collection is obscure, because many parts of the nation don’t maintain data about their collection and processing infrastructure. Of the 59 states and territories that responded to the EPA survey, 48% don’t collect data on the number of curbside programs operating in their jurisdictions, and 37% don’t track drop-off programs. You can’t improve what you don’t measure, and states aren’t measuring.

The Recycling Economics Demand a Modernized System

The $36.5 to $43.4 billion investment needed to make U.S. recycling competitive with the rest of the world falls into two main categories. Roughly $22 to $28 billion would fund curbside collection expansion, drop-off upgrades, and modernization of materials recovery facilities (MRFs) to handle the full range of packaging materials in the waste stream. The remaining $14 to $16 billion would address organics, such composting infrastructure and anaerobic digestion for food scraps and yard waste, which currently account for more than a quarter of what goes to landfill.

What does that investment yield? According to the Recycled Materials Association’s 2024 economic impact study, the existing recycling system already generates $184 billion in annual economic activity. The 175,000 Americans directly employed in the industry average more than $100,000 in total wages and benefits; these are jobs that require skilled labor and tend to stay local. Doubling the system’s capacity would drive job growth and related investments in local collection and processing.

The long-term cost comparison is even more striking. A World Economic Forum analysis projects that continuing with current waste management practices will cost more than $417 billion per year by 2050, a $165 billion increase from 2020. The infrastructure investment the EPA is describing is one-time capital spending. The savings are recurring, and the jobs created are a source of economic opportunity.

Rising Disposal Costs Signal Future Landfill Shortages

The cost side of the equation is moving in recycling’s favor. The average U.S. landfill tipping fee — what municipalities and haulers pay to deposit a ton of waste — reached $62.28 per ton in 2024, a 10% increase from the year before and the steepest annual jump since 2022. In short, we are running out of landfill space. In the Northeast, average tipping fees run over $80 per ton. As disposal costs rise, the cost-benefit analysis for materials recovery improves in proportion, even before accounting for the commodity value of recovered materials.

Tipping fees are a useful market signal pointing to a circular future, when burying or burning waste becomes expensive enough that diverting it becomes economically rational for municipalities. Several cities and counties have found that recycling programs with high diversion rates lower their net waste management costs, even after accounting for the added cost of program operations.

Composting is seeing a similar shift. As landfill operators in high-fee regions turn away organics or charge premium rates for wet material, commercial and municipal composting operations are becoming genuinely competitive alternatives. The infrastructure investment the EPA is describing would accelerate this shift rather than waiting for market pressure to do the work slowly.

What the Money Would Actually Build

It helps to be specific about what “infrastructure investment” means in practice:

MRF upgrades: materials recovery facilities sort and separate mixed recyclables into commodity streams that can be sold for profit. Many existing MRFs were designed for a different mix of materials than what households generate today, such as thinner plastics, smaller form factors, and more flexible film. Optical sorting technology and updated equipment can dramatically improve both recovery rates and material quality, but the capital costs often exceed municipal budgets, even though the programs will turn a profit in the long run..

Curbside collection expansion: in rural areas and low-density suburban zones, the per-household cost of traditional curbside routes is high enough that service has never been offered. Regional collection systems, hub-and-spoke drop-off networks, and consolidation of collection contracts across county lines can make service viable in areas that currently have none.

Organics processing: food scraps and yard waste make up 28% of the U.S. municipal solid waste stream by weight, according to 2018 EPA data, the most recent available from the agency. Composting and anaerobic digestion can divert most of that material while producing either compost for agriculture or biogas for energy. Neither requires advanced technology, just facilities and collection infrastructure that most communities lack.

What You Can Do

Individual recycling behavior matters, but in this case, advocacy for systemic investment matters more. The access gap won’t close through consumer choice alone.

At home: Recycle what your program accepts, correctly. Contamination from wishcycling — putting non-recyclables or dirty materials into curbside bins — drives up processing costs and can render entire loads unusable. Use Earth911’s recycling search to confirm what’s accepted in your area before you bin it.

Recycle what your program accepts, correctly. Contamination from wishcycling — putting non-recyclables or dirty materials into curbside bins — drives up processing costs and can render entire loads unusable. Use Earth911’s recycling search to confirm what’s accepted in your area before you bin it. At the household level: Compost food scraps if your municipality offers pickup or if you have space to do it at home. Diverting organics from the landfill is one of the highest-impact actions available to most households, both to reduce methane emissions and for producing soil amendments that sequester carbon.

Compost food scraps if your municipality offers pickup or if you have space to do it at home. Diverting organics from the landfill is one of the highest-impact actions available to most households, both to reduce methane emissions and for producing soil amendments that sequester carbon. At the community and policy level: Contact your municipal waste management department or local elected officials and ask specifically about infrastructure investment plans. The EPA report gives local advocates a concrete ask: expanded curbside access, MRF modernization funding, and organics collection. Federal infrastructure dollars are available for these projects; the question is whether local officials are pursuing them.

Contact your municipal waste management department or local elected officials and ask specifically about infrastructure investment plans. The EPA report gives local advocates a concrete ask: expanded curbside access, MRF modernization funding, and organics collection. Federal infrastructure dollars are available for these projects; the question is whether local officials are pursuing them. Support extended producer responsibility (EPR) legislation: Several states have passed or are considering laws that shift the costs of recycling infrastructure to producers of packaging and products, rather than placing the full burden on municipal budgets. EPR programs in California, Colorado, Maine, and Oregon are early models worth watching and supporting.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant opportunity for broad positive environmental and economic impact through federal investment in U.S. recycling infrastructure. It details the current shortcomings, the scale of investment needed, and the tangible benefits including job creation, increased recycling rates, reduced landfill use, and long-term cost savings. The focus is clear and well-supported by data, emphasizing systemic improvements with wide societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: Investing in U.S. Recycling Infrastructure Could Double Recycling and Create Jobs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The U.S. recycling system currently recycles about 32% of waste but faces challenges like limited access to recycling services and outdated facilities. The federal government estimates that the infrastructure is only half-ready to handle growing waste.

**What happened** – The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) reported that investing $36.5 to $43.4 billion in recycling infrastructure could nearly double the national recycling rate to 61%. This investment would expand curbside collection, upgrade sorting facilities, and improve composting and organics processing.

**Impact** – This investment would generate more jobs, improve recycling access for underserved communities, and reduce the amount of waste sent to landfills. Currently, the recycling industry supports over 600,000 jobs and creates $184 billion in economic activity. Improving recycling infrastructure would also save money in the long run by lowering waste disposal costs that are rising rapidly.

**What's next step** – Local governments and communities need to pursue federal funds for recycling improvements and support laws that make producers responsible for recycling costs. Individuals can help by recycling correctly and composting food scraps when possible. These actions combined can make recycling easier and more effective for everyone.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Investing in modern recycling infrastructure can boost recycling rates, create jobs, and reduce waste, making it a smart move for both the economy and the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Investir dans les infrastructures de recyclage aux États-Unis pourrait doubler le recyclage et créer des emplois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le système de recyclage américain recycle actuellement environ 32 % des déchets, mais il fait face à des défis comme un accès limité aux services de recyclage et des installations obsolètes. Le gouvernement fédéral estime que les infrastructures sont seulement à moitié prêtes à gérer l’augmentation des déchets.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Agence de protection de l’environnement (EPA) a rapporté qu’un investissement de 36,5 à 43,4 milliards de dollars dans les infrastructures de recyclage pourrait presque doubler le taux national de recyclage à 61 %. Cet investissement permettrait d’étendre la collecte en bord de rue, de moderniser les centres de tri, et d’améliorer le compostage et le traitement des matières organiques.

**Impact** – Cet investissement créerait plus d’emplois, améliorerait l’accès au recyclage pour les communautés mal desservies, et réduirait la quantité de déchets envoyés en décharge. Actuellement, l’industrie du recyclage soutient plus de 600 000 emplois et génère 184 milliards de dollars d’activité économique. Améliorer les infrastructures de recyclage permettrait aussi d’économiser de l’argent à long terme en réduisant les coûts croissants d’élimination des déchets.

**Prochaine étape** – Les gouvernements locaux et les communautés doivent rechercher des fonds fédéraux pour améliorer le recyclage et soutenir les lois qui rendent les producteurs responsables des coûts du recyclage. Les particuliers peuvent aider en recyclant correctement et en compostant les restes alimentaires quand c’est possible. Ces actions combinées peuvent rendre le recyclage plus facile et plus efficace pour tous.

**Une phrase clé** – Investir dans des infrastructures de recyclage modernes peut augmenter les taux de recyclage, créer des emplois, et réduire les déchets, ce qui est une décision intelligente pour l’économie et l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Invertir en infraestructura de reciclaje en EE.UU. podría duplicar el reciclaje y crear empleos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El sistema de reciclaje en Estados Unidos actualmente recicla alrededor del 32% de los desechos, pero enfrenta problemas como acceso limitado a servicios de reciclaje y plantas obsoletas. El gobierno federal estima que las infraestructuras están solo a la mitad de su capacidad para manejar el aumento de residuos.

**Qué pasó** – La Agencia de Protección Ambiental (EPA) informó que invertir entre 36.5 y 43.4 mil millones de dólares en infraestructura de reciclaje podría casi duplicar la tasa nacional de reciclaje al 61%. Esta inversión ampliaría la recolección en la acera, actualizaría las plantas de clasificación y mejoraría el compostaje y el procesamiento de materiales orgánicos.

**Impacto** – Esta inversión generaría más empleos, mejoraría el acceso al reciclaje para comunidades desatendidas y reduciría la cantidad de basura enviada a los vertederos. Actualmente, la industria del reciclaje sostiene más de 600,000 empleos y genera 184 mil millones de dólares en actividad económica. Mejorar la infraestructura de reciclaje también ahorraría dinero a largo plazo al reducir los costos crecientes de eliminación de residuos.

**Próximo paso** – Los gobiernos locales y las comunidades deben buscar fondos federales para mejorar el reciclaje y apoyar leyes que hagan a los productores responsables de los costos del reciclaje. Las personas pueden ayudar reciclando correctamente y compostando los restos de comida cuando sea posible. Estas acciones juntas pueden hacer que el reciclaje sea más fácil y efectivo para todos.

**Una frase clave** – Invertir en infraestructura moderna de reciclaje puede aumentar las tasas de reciclaje, crear empleos y reducir residuos, siendo una decisión inteligente para la economía y el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant community scene featuring stylized recycling bins and compost containers arranged along a peaceful neighborhood street, with interconnected, glowing pathways symbolizing expanded curbside collection and modernized materials recovery facilities, alongside a flourishing garden of composted plants and swirling biogas shapes rising gently, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones.

articles.title: Funding the fight against corporate polluters
articles.content:
For decades, toxic lead cables lay like giant sea monsters snaking across the bottom of Lake Tahoe. Installed as early telecommunications lines and owned by AT&T, the abandoned cables contained over 100,000 pounds of lead and were slowly deteriorating, leaching contaminants into one of the most iconic lakes in the country.

In 2021, the California Sportfishing Protection Alliance, or CSPA, filed a lawsuit to force AT&T to remove the abandoned lead cables. The multimillion-dollar litigation dragged on for over two years. Without sampling and scientific testing, it was difficult to make the case in court. Then Roland Peralta, the founder of a new non-profit called WHEN Justice, offered to provide $100,000 for the science needed to support the litigation. The case quickly changed course.

With the new funding, scuba divers went down to the lake floor to collect samples that helped show that the lead in the cables was leaching into the surrounding waters. The crucial piece of missing evidence was found with lead isotopic testing, providing “fingerprints” that showed the lead in the cables was the same as the surrounding contamination in the lake.

Once the lead contamination was linked to the cables, AT&T settled the case in nine weeks and removed the cables within months. “That was the ‘aha’ moment, where we realized we could scale this model,” said Peralta.

An innovative pay-it-forward model

WHEN Justice is built around a simple but deeply powerful idea: Strategic funding at the right moment in litigation can help public-interest cases win. The non-profit raises funds to help pay for the costly pieces of litigation at the intersection of environmental and human health — like scientific testing, expert analysis, and sampling — that smaller entities taking on massive corporations in court frequently cannot afford. Because environmental lawsuits often include cost recovery, WHEN’s donations can sometimes be used repeatedly, helping fund multiple cases from the same original donation.

Building off the AT&T case success, WHEN was officially launched on Earth Day in April of 2026. “WHEN made a huge difference in the Lake Tahoe case,” said Erica Maharg, the environmental attorney who represented the sportfishing group against AT&T. “Those funds provided us with the evidence we needed at a critical time in the litigation.”

Colin West, founder and CEO of the nonprofit Clean Up the Lake, sees that role as especially important for groups without deep legal budgets. “Being a smaller organization that is trying to step up and take on big litigation against larger entities is challenging,” he said. “A group like WHEN Justice that has closing that funding gap as their core focus fills a need, and a role that others can’t.”

The litigation bottleneck

Adequate funding is critical in today’s environmental justice landscape. Currently, much of the environmental law enforcement that forces powerful entities to clean up pollution is being done by individuals and nonprofit organizations. “We think of government and regulators as being the primary enforcement mechanisms. But in reality, many environmental cleanups happen because of the efforts of small organizations,” said Maharg.

For those groups, the main obstacle is usually not the legal standard, but the cost of assembling the scientific evidence needed to support the case. “If you’re going up against big companies, litigation is so expensive,” said WHEN’s chief executive and legal officer, Jacqueline Biner. “The way the legal system is set up right now, it sadly is not always about the merits of the case. It’s often just about who has more money.”

WHEN’s model is designed to help remove that barrier. In another current campaign, the organization is fundraising to support scientific analysis and sampling in a lawsuit involving a long-closed hazardous waste disposal site located on the edge of the San Pablo Bay in California. The lawsuit alleges that toxic substances are now leaching from the landfill into the bay. Together, WHEN and the public are funding experts to conduct sampling and analyze the results, showing how the pollution is moving from the landfill toward the bay, grounding the case in science and data.

“There are so many cases out there, like landfills that are leaching contaminants, or factory farms leaking manure and nitrates into water bodies,” said Biner. She sees strategic, targeted litigation funding as a tool that could help fuel a sea change, showing large corporations that it’s more cost-efficient to clean up their own messes and address sources of harm, rather than fighting in court to try to evade responsibility.

A new way to demand accountability

A serial entrepreneur and the co-founder of wellness company Nutrafol, WHEN’s founder Roland Peralta initially envisioned giving back through traditional philanthropy. But he soon began to see an additional, larger opportunity: By directing strategic funding to organizations fighting expensive David-and-Goliath battles against large corporations, he could help create a healthier planet for future generations. “I kept asking myself, what kind of world will I leave for my son?” he said.

By helping cover litigation costs, WHEN can help smaller organizations pursue cases against much larger and better-resourced opponents. “The ability to access funding for litigation is really exciting, because it means that we can take on bigger cases,” said Maharg. “Having a partner like WHEN that can help with fundraising key costs in the litigation makes all the difference in our ability to take on these well-funded polluters.”

Over the next several years, WHEN Justice aims to build an infrastructure for a new kind of corporate accountability — helping identify harmful corporate conduct before it becomes irreversible. They plan to give people a direct way to participate in the fight for transparency and justice through crowd-funding. Peralta and Biner envision an interactive platform, where donors can direct their dollars toward the fights that matter most to them. These funds would support both large, precedent-setting cases, and smaller, community-driven campaigns that help local residents confront injustices in their own backyards.

Winning is not the only goal, said Biner. “We also believe it is ok to pursue a case that may lose if it will create momentum and pressure. If we educate the courts, place new accountability on corporations, and educate consumers, that moves the needle in a different way.”

WHEN’s goal is also to get the public involved. “We think there is so much more power in a million people giving a dollar than in a single person giving a million dollars,” Biner noted. “We want people to become invested in these cases, to follow them, and to become more informed about the legal process.”

A call to action

That sense of agency is central to WHEN’s purpose. At its core, the model is designed to give people a concrete, accessible way to respond to injustices that might otherwise feel too large or too entrenched to take on.

For Peralta, that means creating a clear path that allows ordinary people to turn outrage into action. “Everybody’s angry and appalled about these massive environmental issues, but as an individual, it’s hard to know how to help,” he said. “WHEN Justice is designed to be the call to action button that everyone has been longing for, to help individuals play a role in shutting this madness down by voting with their dollar.”

WHEN Justice is a charitable crowdfunding platform for legal and regulatory action at the intersection of environmental and human health. From pollution and toxic exposure to climate-related harms, we select and fund high-impact efforts that seek accountability from powerful actors whose actions put people, communities, and ecosystems at risk. Every campaign is open to the public, creating a direct way for concerned citizens to participate in outcomes that affect their families, communities, and future.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a successful environmental cleanup effort enabled by strategic funding for litigation against corporate polluters, demonstrating a scalable model that empowers smaller organizations to hold large corporations accountable. This has broad significance for environmental justice, public health, and community empowerment, with clear context and details on the innovative funding approach and its positive impact.
articles.personality_title: New nonprofit helps small groups fight big polluters with smart funding
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Lake Tahoe had old lead cables at its bottom that were leaking toxic lead into the water. These cables were left by AT&T many years ago and were causing pollution in a famous lake.

**What happened** – In 2021, the California Sportfishing Protection Alliance (CSPA) sued AT&T to remove these cables. The lawsuit was slow because they lacked scientific proof. Then, WHEN Justice, a new nonprofit, gave $100,000 to fund scientific tests. Divers collected samples, and special tests proved the lead in the cables was polluting the lake. After this, AT&T quickly agreed to remove the cables.

**Impact** – This case showed that giving money at the right time for science can help small groups win against big companies. WHEN Justice uses this idea to fund important parts of environmental lawsuits that are usually too expensive for smaller organizations. This helps bring justice and clean up pollution that might otherwise continue.

**What's next step** – WHEN Justice plans to create a platform where the public can help fund different environmental lawsuits. People will be able to choose which cases to support, helping more groups fight pollution and hold companies responsible. They also want to raise awareness and encourage more public involvement.

**One-sentence takeaway** – WHEN Justice’s smart funding helps small groups gather scientific proof to win lawsuits that clean up pollution and hold big polluters accountable.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle association aide les petits groupes à lutter contre les gros pollueurs grâce à un financement ciblé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le lac Tahoe contenait d’anciens câbles en plomb au fond, qui laissaient fuir du plomb toxique dans l’eau. Ces câbles, abandonnés par AT&T il y a longtemps, polluaient ce lac célèbre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2021, l’Alliance californienne pour la protection de la pêche sportive (CSPA) a poursuivi AT&T pour faire retirer ces câbles. Le procès avançait lentement faute de preuves scientifiques. Puis WHEN Justice, une nouvelle association, a donné 100 000 $ pour financer des tests scientifiques. Des plongeurs ont prélevé des échantillons, et des analyses ont prouvé que le plomb venait bien des câbles. AT&T a alors rapidement accepté de les retirer.

**Impact** – Cette affaire a montré que financer au bon moment la science peut aider les petits groupes à gagner contre les grandes entreprises. WHEN Justice utilise cette idée pour financer des parties essentielles de procès environnementaux, souvent trop coûteuses pour les petites organisations. Cela aide à rendre justice et à nettoyer la pollution.

**Prochaine étape** – WHEN Justice prévoit de créer une plateforme où le public pourra financer différentes poursuites environnementales. Les gens pourront choisir les cas à soutenir, aidant ainsi plus de groupes à lutter contre la pollution et à tenir les entreprises responsables. Ils veulent aussi sensibiliser et encourager plus de participation.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le financement ciblé de WHEN Justice aide les petits groupes à réunir des preuves scientifiques pour gagner des procès qui nettoient la pollution et responsabilisent les gros pollueurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva ONG ayuda a pequeños grupos a enfrentar a grandes contaminadores con financiamiento estratégico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el fondo del lago Tahoe había cables antiguos de plomo que estaban filtrando este metal tóxico al agua. Estos cables, abandonados por AT&T hace muchos años, estaban contaminando este lago famoso.

**Qué pasó** – En 2021, la Alianza de Protección de la Pesca Deportiva de California (CSPA) demandó a AT&T para que retirara los cables. La demanda avanzaba lentamente porque faltaban pruebas científicas. Luego, WHEN Justice, una nueva organización sin fines de lucro, dio 100,000 dólares para financiar pruebas científicas. Buceadores tomaron muestras y pruebas especiales mostraron que el plomo de los cables estaba contaminando el lago. Después, AT&T aceptó rápidamente retirar los cables.

**Impacto** – Este caso mostró que dar dinero en el momento adecuado para la ciencia puede ayudar a grupos pequeños a ganar contra grandes compañías. WHEN Justice usa esta idea para financiar partes importantes de demandas ambientales que suelen ser muy caras para organizaciones pequeñas. Esto ayuda a hacer justicia y limpiar la contaminación.

**Próximo paso** – WHEN Justice planea crear una plataforma donde el público pueda ayudar a financiar diferentes demandas ambientales. Las personas podrán elegir qué casos apoyar, ayudando a más grupos a combatir la contaminación y responsabilizar a las empresas. También quieren aumentar la conciencia y la participación pública.

**Frase clave** – El financiamiento inteligente de WHEN Justice ayuda a grupos pequeños a obtener pruebas científicas para ganar demandas que limpian la contaminación y hacen responsables a los grandes contaminadores.
articles.image_prompt: A serene underwater scene of a crystal-clear lake floor where ancient, rusted lead cables lie partially buried like sleeping sea serpents, surrounded by vibrant fish and healthy aquatic plants gently flourishing, while a glowing, golden light from above symbolizes hope and justice illuminating the water and the cables.

articles.title: Press release - MEPs strengthen the EU’s carbon border adjustment mechanism and close loopholes
articles.summary: Environment Committee MEPs have backed extending the EU's carbon border adjustment mechanism to downstream goods and setting up a fund to support industry's low-carbon transition.Committee on the Environment, Public Health and Food Safety Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
The Committee on the Environment, Climate and Food Safety adopted its position on proposed changes to the CBAM by 56 to 11, with 12 abstentions.

The MEPs agree with the Commission's proposal to extend the scope of CBAM beyond basic materials to a long list of downstream products – finished steel and aluminium goods such as fasteners, wire, springs and household articles – but insist it must be based on transparent, quantitative methodologies. They also added an exemption for electricity flows from non-EU countries used by grid operators to keep networks stable.

Closing loopholes

On anti-circumvention rules, the MEPs clarified that the practice of “slightly modifying” goods must also cover slight processing and tightened the rule so it targets only arrangements set up purely to dodge CBAM, and not normal business decisions to lower a company's costs.

They also empowered the Commission to apply the true country of origin's default values where a pattern of circumvention is found. They deleted the Commission's proposed safeguard that would have allowed goods to be removed from the scope in the event of price shocks.

The committee also wants new rules for online sales to close an online imports loophole. It recommends a single weight-based limit be applied to a seller's shipments as a whole, rather than parcel by parcel, with new reporting duties, and retroactive liability for shipments that are split to stay under the threshold.

Finally, the MEPs propose simplified reporting for least-developed countries and a technical assistance framework, but removed the Commission's option to count Paris Agreement Article 6 carbon credits against CBAM obligations, since this issue is likely to be discussed in the context of the upcoming revision of the EU emissions trading system (ETS).

Temporary decarbonisation fund

The Environment Committee also adopted their position on the related temporary decarbonisation fund (TDF) to protect EU producers on export markets, by 59 votes to 16, with 6 abstentions.

The MEPs want financial support from the TDF to run from 2027 to 2029 and not only from 2028 as proposed by the Commission. As fertilisers are a strategic input for food security, they also want to open the fund to fertiliser producers and downstream users facing higher carbon-related input costs, with products such as urea, ammonium nitrate and ammonium sulphate added to the list of eligible goods.

Finally, all downstream operators – firms that use CBAM-covered goods as inputs in their production – should be eligible for support from the fund, while leftover revenue could be redirected to the EU's international climate finance commitments under the Paris Agreement instead of being returned to member states, as the Commission proposed.

Quotes

CBAM rapporteur Mohammed Chahim (S&D, NL) said: "This compromise makes the CBAM stronger, fairer and more resilient. We have closed important loopholes, strengthened enforcement against circumvention, and expanded the mechanism’s scope where it matters most. It is a balanced package that protects European industry as it decarbonises while safeguarding the environmental integrity of the mechanism."

Rapporteur for the temporary decarbonisation fund Pascal Canfin (Renew, FR) said: "Today we have taken a big step towards making Europe a safe place for investment in decarbonisation: we are broadening product coverage to enhance the level playing field and we are setting out stronger anti-circumvention rules, notably against resource shuffling from China. We are also offering a more robust solution for our farmers hit by fertiliser costs, and an export scheme to protect our companies on export markets where their competitors do not pay a carbon price."

Next steps

Parliament is scheduled to adopt its mandate for negotiations with EU member states on the final shape of the bill during the September plenary session.

Background

The EU’s carbon border adjustment mechanism is the EU's tool to equalise the price of carbon paid for EU products operating under the ETS with that of imported goods, to reduce the risk of carbon leakage and to encourage greater climate ambition in non-EU countries. In 2025, Parliament adopted simplification measures to exempt 90% of importers from CBAM rules while still maintaining climate ambition, as 99% of CO2 emissions are still covered.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the EU Parliament's Environment Committee strengthening the carbon border adjustment mechanism (CBAM) to broaden its scope, close loopholes, and support industry decarbonization through a temporary fund. This represents a significant policy advancement with broad environmental impact, promoting climate action and fair carbon pricing on imports, which benefits the environment and global climate efforts. The article provides detailed context on the legislative changes and their intended effects, demonstrating substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: EU strengthens carbon border rules to support cleaner industry and fair trade
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
The European Union has a system called the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM). It helps make sure that imported products pay a fair price for the carbon pollution they cause, just like products made in the EU. This aims to reduce pollution and stop companies from moving to countries with looser climate rules.

**What happened**
The EU Parliament’s Environment Committee voted to make CBAM stronger and cover more products, including finished steel and aluminium goods like springs and household items. They also added rules to stop companies from avoiding the carbon charges by slightly changing products or splitting shipments online. A fund was created to help EU industries, including fertilizer producers, pay for switching to cleaner methods. The fund will start giving money from 2027.

**Impact**
These changes close loopholes that allowed some companies to avoid paying for their carbon emissions. By including more products and tightening rules, the EU makes sure the system is fairer and more effective. The new fund supports businesses as they reduce pollution, helping protect jobs and the environment. This approach encourages cleaner production both inside and outside the EU.

**What's next step**
The full EU Parliament will discuss and approve these changes in September. Then, they will negotiate with EU member countries to finalize the rules. After that, the strengthened CBAM and the support fund will begin to be put into action.

**One-sentence takeaway**
The EU is improving its carbon border rules to make trade fairer and help industries become cleaner while protecting the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’UE renforce les règles carbone aux frontières pour soutenir une industrie plus propre et un commerce équitable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
L’Union européenne dispose d’un système appelé Mécanisme d’ajustement carbone aux frontières (MACF). Il vise à faire en sorte que les produits importés paient un prix juste pour la pollution carbone qu’ils génèrent, tout comme les produits fabriqués dans l’UE. Cela permet de réduire la pollution et d’éviter que les entreprises ne s’installent dans des pays aux règles climatiques moins strictes.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Le comité de l’Environnement du Parlement européen a voté pour renforcer le MACF et étendre sa portée à davantage de produits, y compris des produits finis en acier et aluminium comme les ressorts et articles ménagers. Des règles ont été ajoutées pour empêcher les entreprises d’éviter les taxes carbone en modifiant légèrement les produits ou en divisant les envois en ligne. Un fonds a été créé pour aider les industries européennes, y compris les producteurs d’engrais, à financer leur transition vers des méthodes plus propres. Le fonds commencera à verser des aides dès 2027.

**Impact**
Ces changements ferment des failles qui permettaient à certaines entreprises d’échapper à la taxe carbone. En incluant plus de produits et en renforçant les règles, l’UE rend le système plus juste et efficace. Le nouveau fonds soutient les entreprises dans leur réduction de pollution, ce qui protège les emplois et l’environnement. Cette démarche encourage une production plus propre en Europe et au-delà.

**Prochaine étape**
Le Parlement européen examinera et approuvera ces modifications en septembre. Ensuite, il négociera avec les États membres pour finaliser les règles. Une fois cela fait, le MACF renforcé et le fonds de soutien seront mis en place.

**Résumé en une phrase**
L’UE améliore ses règles carbone aux frontières pour rendre le commerce plus équitable et aider les industries à devenir plus propres tout en protégeant l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE refuerza las reglas de carbono en las fronteras para apoyar una industria más limpia y un comercio justo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
La Unión Europea tiene un sistema llamado Mecanismo de Ajuste en la Frontera del Carbono (CBAM, por sus siglas en inglés). Este sistema asegura que los productos importados paguen un precio justo por la contaminación de carbono que generan, igual que los productos hechos en la UE. Esto ayuda a reducir la contaminación y evita que las empresas se trasladen a países con normas climáticas más débiles.

**Qué pasó**
El Comité de Medio Ambiente del Parlamento Europeo votó para fortalecer el CBAM y ampliar su alcance a más productos, incluyendo bienes terminados de acero y aluminio como resortes y artículos para el hogar. También añadieron reglas para evitar que las empresas evadan los cargos de carbono modificando ligeramente productos o dividiendo envíos en línea. Se creó un fondo para ayudar a las industrias de la UE, incluyendo productores de fertilizantes, a financiar su transición hacia métodos más limpios. El fondo comenzará a dar apoyo desde 2027.

**Impacto**
Estos cambios cierran lagunas que permitían a algunas empresas evitar pagar por sus emisiones de carbono. Al incluir más productos y endurecer las reglas, la UE hace que el sistema sea más justo y efectivo. El nuevo fondo apoya a las empresas para reducir la contaminación, protegiendo empleos y el medio ambiente. Este enfoque fomenta una producción más limpia dentro y fuera de la UE.

**Próximo paso**
El Parlamento Europeo discutirá y aprobará estos cambios en septiembre. Luego negociará con los países miembros para finalizar las reglas. Después, el CBAM fortalecido y el fondo de apoyo comenzarán a implementarse.

**Frase clave**
La UE mejora sus reglas de carbono en las fronteras para hacer el comercio más justo y ayudar a las industrias a ser más limpias mientras protege el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a balanced scale holding stylized steel and aluminium goods on one side and glowing green energy symbols on the other, set against a backdrop of interconnected, transparent grids representing clear rules and closed loopholes, with subtle hints of flowing electricity and a protective shield encircling the scene to symbolize strengthened climate safeguards and fair trade.

articles.title: Slug sleuths on the trail of gorging gastropods
articles.summary: How predicting slugs' behaviour is helping farmers and their crops.
articles.content:
Arable farmers may soon be able to stop slugs eating their crops thanks to research by a Shropshire-based university and the work of "slug sleuth" farmers.

Data collected by 28 "slug sleuths" has helped researchers from Harper Adams University find environmentally friendly slug control methods in the form of "prediction maps".

It is part of a three-year, £2.6 million research programme that was funded by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).

Researchers hope the maps could lead to "more precise control of slugs in arable fields" and reduce using pesticides.

The model is one of the many projects done by the "Strategies Leading to Improved Management and Enhanced Resilience to Slugs" (SLIMERS).

Professor Keith Walters from the university said the research suggested where slugs were more likely to show up, in particular how slug clusters re-established themselves after soil was waterlogged.

"We have now confirmed that patches reform temporarily in places we wouldn't expect in normal conditions and then quickly return to their predicted areas once more typical soil conditions return," Walters added.

"Despite low slug numbers over the testing period we have sufficient data to prove that the model works, and perhaps most importantly, that farmers are happy to use it and it fits in with modern commercial equipment."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant research breakthrough that enables environmentally friendly slug control in agriculture, reducing pesticide use and benefiting crop production. The impact is broad, aiding farmers and promoting sustainable farming practices. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a funded research program with practical applications.
articles.personality_title: New maps help farmers protect crops from slugs without harmful chemicals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Slugs can damage crops by eating them, causing problems for farmers who grow food on large fields. Finding ways to control slugs without using many pesticides is a challenge.

**What happened** – Researchers at Harper Adams University in Shropshire worked with 28 farmers, called “slug sleuths,” to collect data about where slugs live and move in fields. This research is part of a three-year project funded by the UK government’s Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). The team created special “prediction maps” that show where slugs are most likely to appear.

**Impact** – These maps help farmers know exactly where slugs gather, so they can target those areas instead of spraying pesticides everywhere. This means less chemical use, which is better for the environment and safer for crops. The research also showed how slug groups change after heavy rain, helping farmers understand slug behavior in different weather.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to continue improving the maps and hope more farmers will use this method. The goal is to make slug control easier, cheaper, and greener, helping farmers protect their crops while caring for the land.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists and farmers are working together to use smart maps that predict slug activity, allowing safer and more precise crop protection without relying heavily on pesticides.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles cartes aident les agriculteurs à protéger leurs cultures des limaces sans produits chimiques nocifs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les limaces peuvent endommager les cultures en les mangeant, ce qui pose des problèmes aux agriculteurs qui cultivent de grandes surfaces. Trouver des moyens de contrôler les limaces sans utiliser trop de pesticides est un défi.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université Harper Adams à Shropshire ont travaillé avec 28 agriculteurs, appelés « détectives de limaces », pour collecter des données sur l’emplacement et le déplacement des limaces dans les champs. Cette recherche fait partie d’un projet de trois ans financé par le ministère britannique de l’Environnement, de l’Alimentation et des Affaires rurales (Defra). L’équipe a créé des « cartes de prédiction » spéciales qui montrent où les limaces sont le plus susceptibles d’apparaître.

**Impact** – Ces cartes aident les agriculteurs à savoir précisément où se rassemblent les limaces, afin de cibler ces zones au lieu de pulvériser des pesticides partout. Cela signifie moins d’utilisation de produits chimiques, ce qui est meilleur pour l’environnement et plus sûr pour les cultures. La recherche a aussi montré comment les groupes de limaces changent après de fortes pluies, aidant les agriculteurs à comprendre leur comportement selon la météo.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’améliorer encore les cartes et espèrent que davantage d’agriculteurs utiliseront cette méthode. L’objectif est de rendre le contrôle des limaces plus facile, moins cher et plus écologique, aidant les agriculteurs à protéger leurs cultures tout en respectant la terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques et des agriculteurs collaborent pour utiliser des cartes intelligentes qui prédisent l’activité des limaces, permettant une protection des cultures plus sûre et plus précise sans dépendre beaucoup des pesticides.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos mapas ayudan a agricultores a proteger cultivos de babosas sin productos químicos dañinos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las babosas pueden dañar los cultivos al comerlos, lo que causa problemas a los agricultores que trabajan en grandes campos. Encontrar formas de controlar las babosas sin usar muchos pesticidas es un reto.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Harper Adams en Shropshire trabajaron con 28 agricultores, llamados “detectives de babosas”, para recopilar datos sobre dónde viven y se mueven las babosas en los campos. Esta investigación forma parte de un proyecto de tres años financiado por el Departamento de Medio Ambiente, Alimentación y Asuntos Rurales del Reino Unido (Defra). El equipo creó “mapas de predicción” que muestran dónde es más probable encontrar babosas.

**Impacto** – Estos mapas ayudan a los agricultores a saber exactamente dónde se juntan las babosas, para que puedan tratar esas áreas específicas en lugar de usar pesticidas en todo el campo. Esto significa menos uso de químicos, lo que es mejor para el medio ambiente y más seguro para los cultivos. La investigación también mostró cómo cambian los grupos de babosas después de lluvias fuertes, ayudando a los agricultores a entender su comportamiento según el clima.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir mejorando los mapas y esperan que más agricultores usen este método. La meta es hacer que el control de babosas sea más fácil, barato y ecológico, ayudando a proteger los cultivos y cuidar la tierra.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos y agricultores trabajan juntos para usar mapas inteligentes que predicen la actividad de las babosas, permitiendo una protección más segura y precisa de los cultivos sin depender mucho de pesticidas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gently rolling arable field with subtle clusters of stylized slugs forming glowing, translucent shapes on the soil surface, overlaid with delicate, hand-drawn map lines and markers resembling a natural, flowing network that symbolizes prediction and monitoring, all rendered in soft earth tones and muted greens.
